elements.sent-2411-001: School of Science and Technology University of Tsukuba Tsukuba, Japan masuya.nozomu.sm@alumni.tsukuba.ac.jp \and \IEEEauthorblockN{Sho Sakaino \IEEEauthorblockA{Systems and Information Engineering University of Tsukuba Tsukuba, Japan sakaino@iit.tsukuba.ac.jp \and \IEEEauthorblockN{Toshiaki Tsuji \IEEEauthorblockA{Grad.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: School of Science and Engineering Saitama University Saitama, Japan tsuji@ees.saitama-u.ac.jp.
elements.sent-2411-003: Conventional methods of imitation learning for variable-speed motion have difficulty extrapolating speeds because they rely on learning models running at a constant sampling frequency.
elements.sent-2411-004: This study proposes variable-frequency imitation learning~(VFIL), a novel method for imitation learning with learning models trained to run at variable sampling frequencies along with the desired speeds of motion.
elements.sent-2411-005: The experimental results showed that the proposed method improved the velocity-wise accuracy along both the interpolated and extrapolated frequency labels, in addition to a 12.5~\% increase in the overall success rate.
elements.sent-2411-006: The demand for human-like, cooperative, adaptive, and quick robot manipulation has increased, especially in fields suffering from a shortage of workers.
elements.sent-2411-007: To achieve such manipulation of robots without hard coding, machine learning-based methods are gaining attention.
elements.sent-2411-008: Reinforcement learning-based methods~\cite{levine2018, daydreamer are completely free of prior modeling and teaching.}
elements.sent-2411-009: These methods have proven to be effective in real-world manipulation tasks, although at the cost of enormous trial and error, which causes wear in robots.
elements.sent-2411-010: By contrast, imitation learning~\cite{yang2017, aloha has enabled robots to mimic human motion from human demonstrations, with machine learning models generating command values in accordance with sensory data.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Learning motions at variable-speed settings contributes to a more precise understanding of the dynamics of robots and the surrounding environment.
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite the recent shift toward world models~\cite{guan2024, mitrokhov2024, daydreamer for artificial general intelligence, including general robot manipulation, model-free imitation learning methods for generating variable-speed motions suffer from difficulties in extrapolating the speeds because the variable-speed task is perceived as a multi-task setting.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This study proposes variable-frequency imitation learning~(VFIL), a model-free imitation learning method that considers variable-speed tasks as single-task settings by changing the sampling frequency of the neural network~(NN) models according to the frequency command of the ongoing motion.
elements.sent-2411-014: The proposed method was experimentally verified on a contact-rich variable-speed wiping task.
elements.sent-2411-015: The results showed that the proposed method has a high accuracy in terms of speed and a high success rate, especially in high-frequency settings.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Extraction of Skills from Variable-Speed Demonstrations
elements.sent-2411-016: Time-series alignment has long been a field of research to analyze time-series data more concisely.
elements.sent-2411-017: In robotics, the extraction of skills from demonstrations is one of its use cases.
elements.sent-2411-018: Cohen~et al. proposed Gromov’s dynamic time warping~\cite{cohen2021 , which is an extension of dynamic time warping~\cite{sakoe1978 , and verified it in imitation learning settings using time-series generative adversarial networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, Rasines~et al. extended dynamic time warping and generated smooth trajectories from datasets obtained from real-world robots~\cite{rasines2023 , whereas the generated trajectories were not verified in an actual environment.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Methods based on dynamic movement primitives~\cite{saveriano2023 are also used for learning from demonstrations using a variety of time series.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Xu~et al. achieved a shorter execution time and lower acceleration in a water-carrying task through nonlinear speed scaling~\cite{XU2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Although these approaches aim to extract skills from demonstrations with different timings, they do not focus on generating motion at variable speeds or adaptation on a real-time basis with a feedback loop.
elements.sent-2411-023: This can lead to limited adaptability to variable-speed dynamic motions whose commands can vary according to their frequencies.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Generation of Variable-Speed Motion from Demonstrations
elements.sent-2411-024: Although humans can easily adjust the speed of motion of everyday activities, including handling unknown objects or conducting contact-rich tasks, a method to achieve variable-speed motion in hard-to-simulate robot manipulation has not been developed.
elements.sent-2411-025: Under assumptions including negligible inertial forces, Perico~et al. achieved variable-speed motion through probabilistic principal component analysis~\cite{perico2020 in the settings of learning from demonstrations~\cite{perico2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Although this method can generate motion at variable settings using relatively digestible algorithms, the assumptions behind the method render it inapplicable to high-speed motion, including that at high accelerations.
elements.sent-2411-027: In addition, under the assumption that environmental changes are negligible, variable-speed teaching playback with position-force control developed by Yokokura~et al.~\cite{yokokura2009, fujisaki2023 can also be regarded as the achievement of variable-speed motion from demonstrations.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Without these assumptions, Sakaino~et al. verified the feasibility of variable-speed motion in imitation learning by using neural network~(NN) models and force control~\cite{sakaino2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Saigusa~et al. implemented self-supervised learning using this method~\cite{CRANEX7params to achieve time-wise accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Although the fine-tuning method contributed to improved accuracy for interpolations, extrapolations to unseen speeds remain to be achieved, with their NN models trained at a constant sampling frequency, regardless of the frequency of the robots' actions.
elements.sent-2411-031: This study distinguishes itself by changing the sampling frequency of the NN model according to the command value of the frequency of motion as a velocity-wise inductive bias.
elements.sent-2411-032: In addition, in the context of data augmentation, velocity-wise transformations are considered to increase the diversity of datasets.
elements.sent-2411-033: The model proposed by Yamamoto~et al. could select various objects from a conveyor belt moving at variable speeds by diversifying the speed and phase of the motion data obtained from three human demonstrations via downsampling~\cite{yamamoto2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Although this method achieved end-to-end motion generation by adapting to a variable-speed environment, it did not focus on changing the speed in accordance with external commands.
elements.sent-2411-035: This study proposes variable-frequency imitation learning~(VFIL), a novel method of imitation learning with time-wise normalization and learning models running at variable frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-036: The flow of the VFIL is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:proposal .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In conventional imitation learning methods, the sampling frequencies of the learning models remain unchanged between the training data.
elements.sent-2411-038: When learning variable-speed motions, trajectories are acquired at various frequencies, and motion is learned at a constant sampling frequency, forcing the learning model to adapt to environmental changes and generate different motions at variable speeds.
elements.sent-2411-039: This complicates the learning process as simple changes in speed convert the problem of learning variable-speed motion into multi-task learning problems, leading to poor performance, especially in extrapolations.
elements.sent-2411-040: In contrast, through time-wise normalization, the proposed method treats the changes in the time domain and the related nonlinearity, equally as changes in environments and robot systems, such as rough surfaces, heavy frames, or worn gears.
elements.sent-2411-041: Under this inductive bias, the model simply learns to be robust against nonlinear changes, being free from following multiple trajectories in time series.
elements.sent-2411-042: The pseudo-codes for time-wise normalization and denormalization are shown in Algorithms~\ref{alg1 and \ref{alg2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In this method, $N$ demonstrations of training data with a sampling frequency $F$ and a variety of motion frequencies $f_1, f_2, \cdots, f_N$ were resampled to a constant motion frequency $f_0$ with labels indicating the original speeds.
elements.sent-2411-044: To minimize the obvious differences between the training data, the velocity data were scaled by factors of $f_0 / f_1, f_0 / f_2, \cdots f_0 / f_N$.
elements.sent-2411-045: This time-wise normalization enables the training of the learning model at different sampling frequencies, $f_1 F / f_0, f_2 F / f_0, \cdots f_N F/ f_0$, for each demonstration with different environmental reactions according to the frequency labels $f_1, f_2, \cdots, f_N$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The inference was conducted in real time according to the desired motion frequency $f$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The learning model was given the frequency label $f$ and was run at the average sampling frequency of $fF/f_0$ in addition to the velocity data scaled at $f/f_0$ as time-wise denormalization.
elements.sent-2411-048: To preserve the control system across all variable-frequency motions, the sampling rate of the control system was kept constant and higher than that of the learning model.
elements.sent-2411-049: Fig.~\ref{fig:contloop depicts the adjustment of the sampling times between the learning model and the control loop.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the inference phase, the progression of the learning model to the next step is determined at each step of the control system.
elements.sent-2411-051: The learning model moves to the next step when the sum of the elapsed time from the model's last step $t$ and the remainder from the second last step $t_r$ exceeds $f_0/fF$.
elements.sent-2411-052: The remaining time $t - f_0/fF$ is passed on to the nest step as $t_r$.
elements.sent-2411-053: To calculate the time required for the learning model, the inputs and outputs were obtained at different time steps.
elements.figure-2411-001: \subfloat[Conventional method]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.6, bb = 0 0 788 407]{conventional_.pdf}} \label{conventional} } \subfloat[Variable-frequency imitation learning]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.6, bb = 0 0 775 779]{proposal3.pdf}} \label{proposal} } \caption{Flow of the conventional and the proposed methods.} \label{fig:proposal}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{algorithm}[H] \caption{Time-series normalization} \label{alg1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $f_0 \gets base\ motion\ frequency$ \ForAll{demonstration data with motion frequency $f_i$} \State Resample demonstration at $(f_0 / f_i)$ times the speed \State $ velocity \gets velocity \times (f_0 / f_i)$ \EndFor \Return{training data} \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{algorithm}[H] \caption{Time-series denormalization along with the control loop} \label{alg2} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Initialize $t, t_r$ \State $t_s \gets control\ loop's\ sampling\ time$ \State $F \gets learning\ model's\ frequency$ \State $f_0 \gets base\ motion\ frequency$ \State $f \gets task\ motion\ frequency$ \While{the task is ongoing} \State $(\bold{\theta}^{res}, \bold{\omega}^{res}, \bold{\tau}^{res}) \gets robot's\ response$ \Comment (angle,~angular~velocity,~torque) \If{$t + t_r \geq f_0/fF$} \State $ \bold{\omega}^{res} \gets \bold{\omega}^{res} \times (f_0 / f)$ \State send $\bold{\theta}^{res}, \bold{\omega}^{res}, \bold{\tau}^{res}$ to the learning model \State $t_r \gets t - f_0/fF$ \State $t \gets 0$ \EndIf \If{the next step of sending} \State $(\bold{\theta}^{cmd}, \bold{\omega}^{cmd}, \bold{\tau}^{cmd}) \gets robot's\ command$ \Comment (angle,~angular~velocity,~torque) \State $\bold{\omega}^{cmd} \gets \bold{\omega}^{cmd} \times (f / f_0)$ \State update the command value with $\bold{\theta}^{cmd}, \bold{\omega}^{cmd}, \bold{\tau}^{cmd}$ \EndIf \State Calculate the control input \State Input the control input to the robot \State $t \gets t + t_s$ \State wait for the remainder of the control cycle \EndWhile \end{algorithmic} \end{algorithm}
elements.figure-2411-004: \centerline{\includegraphics[scale=0.25, bb=0 0 904 441]{contloop_.pdf}} \caption{Adjustment of time steps between the learning model and control loop.} \label{fig:contloop}
elements.sent-2411-054: We evaluated the proposed method using bilateral control-based imitation learning~\cite{adachi2018, CRANEX7params along with the conventional method with a constant sampling frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In bilateral-control-based imitation learning, human demonstrations are collected through four-channel bilateral control~\cite{sakaino2011 , which is a teleoperation method with symmetrical position-force feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The objective of the four-channel bilateral control is to satisfy the following equations: \begin{equation}\bold{\theta}^\mathrm{res}_f - \bold{\theta}^\mathrm{res}_l = \bold {0}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\bold{\tau}^\mathrm{res}_f + \bold{\tau}^\mathrm{res}_l = \bold {0},\end{equation} between the leader robot on the operator’s side and the follower robot physically conducting the task.
elements.sent-2411-057: A block diagram of the four-channel bilateral control system is shown in Fig.~\ref{4ch .}
elements.sent-2411-058: In both the equations and block diagrams, the variables $\bold{\theta}$, $\bold{\omega}$ and $\bold{\tau}$ denote the vectors of the joint angle, angular velocity, and torque, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: The superscript $ref$ indicates the reference value, and $res$ indicates the response value.
elements.sent-2411-060: The subscripts $l$ and $f$ refer to the leader and follower, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-061: After training the NN model, the motion was reproduced using the NN model instead of the leader robot, as shown in Fig.~\ref{moho .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The circumflex represents the value estimated using the NN model.
elements.figure-2411-005: \subfloat[Four-channel bilateral control]{ \resizebox*{6cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.5, bb = 0 0 718 324]{4ch.pdf}} \label{4ch} } \subfloat[Bilateral control-based imitation learning]{ \resizebox*{6cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.5, bb = 0 0 704 315]{moho.pdf}} \label{moho} } \caption{Block diagrams of four-channel bilateral control and bilateral control-based imitation learning.} \label{fig:4chmoho}
elements.sent-2411-063: To evaluate the stability and adaptability of the proposed method, we prepared the setup for a wiping task, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:task .}
elements.sent-2411-064: In this task, the robot must first detect the height of the wiping surface by pressing the eraser onto the whiteboard.
elements.sent-2411-065: It should then grasp the eraser and wipe the board continuously at a prescribed frequency.
elements.sent-2411-066: Each trial lasted 40 s, including the pressing, grasping, and wiping tasks.
elements.sent-2411-067: A trial was considered to have failed if the eraser lost contact with the whiteboard at any time after the pressing phase.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the teaching process, 18 demonstrations were collected through teleoperation, three times each at 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8~Hz and at heights of 10 and 15~cm.
elements.sent-2411-069: To collect data at constant frequencies, demonstrations were conducted with the metronome set at 48, 72, and 96 beats per minute, moving either forward or backward at each beat.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the trials, the commands for the wiping frequencies were set to the unlearned 0.2, 1.0, and 1.4~Hz, in addition to the learned 0.6~Hz.
elements.figure-2411-006: \subfloat[Initial state]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.15, bb = 0 0 1920 1080]{wp1.jpg}} } \subfloat[Pressing]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.15, bb = 0 0 1920 1080]{wp2.jpg}} } \subfloat[Grasping]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.15, bb = 0 0 1920 1080]{wp3.jpg}} } \subfloat[Wiping]{ \resizebox*{8cm}{!}{\includegraphics[scale=0.15, bb = 0 0 1920 1080]{wp4.jpg}} } \caption{Procedure of the wiping task taught through bilateral control.} \label{fig:task}
elements.sent-2411-071: We used two CRANE-X7 manipulators~(RT Corp., Tokyo, Japan), as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:crane .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Each manipulator had an arm with seven degrees of freedom (DoF) and a rigid end effector with one DoF.
elements.sent-2411-073: The third joint of the arms was constrained to eliminate the redundant DoFs and the end effectors were replaced with the cross-structured hand developed by Yamane et al.~\cite{yamaneral .}
elements.figure-2411-007: \centerline{\includegraphics[scale=0.064, bb=0 0 2647 2118]{IMG_1532a.jpg}} \caption{CRANE-X7 manipulator with a cross-structured hand.} \label{fig:crane}
elements.sent-2411-074: To enable torque sensing without torque sensors, each joint of the manipulator was controlled with a disturbance observer~(DOB) and reaction force observer~(RFOB), as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:controller , with parameters identical to those used by Saigusa et al.~\cite{CRANEX7params .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The variables ${\theta}$, ${\omega}$, and ${\tau}$ denote the joint angle, angular velocity, and torque, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-076: The superscripts $cmd$, $ref$, $res$, and $dis$ denote command, reference, response, and disturbance, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-077: The circumflexes~(~$\hat{}$~) denote the DOB estimates.
elements.figure-2411-008: \centerline{\includegraphics[scale=0.26, bb=0 0 966 319]{controller2.pdf}} \caption{Block diagram of the controller for each joint of the manipulators.} \label{fig:controller}
elements.sent-2411-078: The neural network (NN) model shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:nnmodel consisted of eight long short-term memory (LSTM)~\cite{LSTM1, LSTM2 layers with 200 dimensions and a fully connected (FC) layer before the output layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The model had 22 dimensions, including the follower's joint angles, angular velocities, and torques, in addition to the real-world frequency of the wiping motion.
elements.sent-2411-080: The outputs had 42 dimensions consisting of both the follower's and leader's states.
elements.sent-2411-081: The configuration of the inputs and outputs was based on the follower-to-follower/leader setting~\cite{F2FL, akagawaJIA , enabling the prediction of both the command and environmental reaction and making validation through autoregression possible.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The superscripts $cmd$, $ref$, $res$ and $dis$ denote command, reference, response, and disturbance, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-083: The sampling frequency of the NN model, $F$, was set to 25~Hz.
elements.sent-2411-084: For the training data without VFIL, all the 500~Hz demonstration data were downsampled to 20 episodes each~\cite{yamaneral, rahmatizadeh2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-085: For the training data with VFIL, $f_0$ was set to 0.6~Hz, and the data with a wiping frequency $f$ of 0.6~Hz were treated in the same manner as those without VFIL.
elements.sent-2411-086: Wiping data with $f$ values of 0.4~Hz and 0.8~Hz were first resampled to 333.3~Hz and 666.7~Hz using linear interpolation and then downsampled to 20 episodes each, with the sampling frequency $fF/f_0$ at 16.7~Hz and 33.3~Hz, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-087: Table~\ref{vfilcfil shows the success rates for the wiping task.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The proposed method achieved higher success rates than the conventional method for both high- and low-height settings, contributing to a 12.5~\% increase in the total success rate.
elements.sent-2411-089: All failures were due to the loss of contact during the wiping motion in the low-height setting.
elements.sent-2411-090: This is because loss of contact is unlikely when the surface is closer to the robot than predicted, and the NN models have learned to adapt to such closeness.
elements.sent-2411-091: In all failed trials, the wiping motion continued even after the loss of contact, and the contacts were recovered.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Success Rate} \begin{center} \subfloat[With Variable-Frequency Imitation Learning]{ \resizebox*{\hsize}{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Height \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Frequency Label (Hz)} (cm) \textbf{0.2} \textbf{0.6} \textbf{1.0} \textbf{1.4} \textbf{Total} 15 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 20/20(100\%) 10 1/5(20\%) 3/5(60\%) 5/5(80\%) 5/5(100\%) 14/20(70\%) Total 6/10(60\%) 8/10(80\%) 10/10(100\%) 10/10(100\%) 34/40(85\%) \end{tabular} } \label{vfil} } \end{center} \begin{center} \subfloat[Without Variable-Frequency Imitation Learning]{ \resizebox*{\hsize}{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Height \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{Frequency Label (Hz)} (cm) \textbf{0.2} \textbf{0.6} \textbf{1.0} \textbf{1.4} \textbf{Total} 15 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 5/5(100\%) 20/20(100\%) 10 3/5(60\%) 3/5(60\%) 1/5(20\%) 2/5(40\%) 9/20(45\%) Total 8/10(80\%) 8/10(80\%) 6/10(60\%) 7/10(70\%) 29/40(73\%) \end{tabular} } \label{cfil} } \end{center} \label{vfilcfil}
elements.sent-2411-092: Figs.~\ref{fig:vfil12 ~and~\ref{fig:cfil40 depict the actual frequency of the wiping motion in successful trials with and without VFIL.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The proposed method contributed to a smaller variance from the frequency label, even at untaught frequencies, whereas the conventional method failed to perform outside the frequencies in the teaching data, that is, between 0.4 and 0.8~Hz.
elements.sent-2411-094: The larger variance at 1.4~Hz with VFIL was likely due to increased friction and other nonlinearities in the robot system, indicating that the success of the task was prioritized over the frequencies of the motions.
elements.figure-2411-011: \centerline{\includegraphics[scale=0.7, bb=0 0 360 219]{vfil12a.pdf}} \caption{Actual frequencies of successful trials with VFIL. Data being closer to the dashed line indicates a better fit.} \label{fig:vfil12}
elements.figure-2411-012: \centerline{\includegraphics[scale=0.7, bb=0 0 360 219]{cfil40.pdf}} \caption{Actual frequency of successful trials without VFIL. Data being closer to the dashed line indicates a better fit.} \label{fig:cfil40}
elements.sent-2411-095: Improvements in both the success rate and accuracy suggest that the proposed method succeeded in generating variable-speed motion, even outside the range of training data.
elements.sent-2411-096: In the high-frequency motion, the success of the task was prioritized over moving quickly and failing, although there were some delays.
elements.sent-2411-097: This prioritization is unlikely in simple playbacks, and delays can be overcome by fine-tuning using self-supervised learning~\cite{CRANEX7params .}
elements.sent-2411-098: The increased number of failures in the low-frequency setting is likely due to the increased sampling time of the NN model, which reached 120~ms at a wiping frequency $f =$~0.2~Hz.
elements.sent-2411-099: Below describes the limitations of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-100: First, in this method, there is a tradeoff between the feasibilities of faster and slower motions.
elements.sent-2411-101: This is because the interval of the NN model is given solely by $f_0/fF$, and because of the complexity of the NN model, certain calculation time is required at each step.
elements.sent-2411-102: A smaller sampling frequency $F$ enables higher-frequency motion with a larger $f/f_0$, which leads to a longer $f_0/fF$ and a jaggier motion at a lower motion frequency $f$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Training data with different base frequencies $f_0$ may enable both, although this leads to multi-task learning settings and may affect the success rate and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-104: The interpolation of samples in a predictive manner can resolve this issue.
elements.sent-2411-105: Second, because this method requires a frequency label prior to the inference phase, the frequency of motion does not change autonomously.
elements.sent-2411-106: Our future work will include the use of a hierarchical architecture~\cite{kaisogata to determine the velocity of movement according to the situation.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This paper proposes variable-frequency imitation learning, a novel imitation learning method with time-wise normalization that enables inference at variable frequencies according to the frequency of motion.
elements.sent-2411-108: By adding this inductive bias, the learning model tasks are reduced by generating a variable-speed motion to adapt to the additional nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-109: The experimental results of the wiping task show a remarkable improvement in accuracy along the frequency label and an increased success rate in high-frequency settings.
elements.sent-2411-110: Our future work will involve widening the frequency range and enabling autonomous decisions regarding the frequency of motion.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
